speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape 3, interviewing Phyllis Cooper Young. Today is November 6, 1995. Phyllis, we were talking about the people in the factory. You mentioned there were French prisoners of war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who else worked in this factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's us. And like I-- they had a few German civilians working there.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And French prisoners of war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of contact did you have with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Young people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Young people, right? What can I tell you? I don't know how we even communicated. I certainly-- to this day, I don't know French, and I don't care.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you communicate? What was the interaction?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a couple woman-man interaction, believe it or not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sexual contact?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know that it went that far, because there was just no place to do-- [LAUGHS]-- to do it. But the feelings-- the feelings were there, absolutely. Feelings were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let's talk about the conditions in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible in that--

speaker: interviewer
text: --of [? Haza. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, [? Haza. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Haza. ?] What were the sleeping accommodations?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think triple bunks we had at that point already.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many women in each bunk?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't tell you this, because they kept constantly sending us people. As a matter of fact--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you sleep alone? Or did you have other women sleeping next to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, alone-- alone-- on a straw--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mattress type?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mattress, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any personal belongings, like photographs or mementos?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's-- that's what you-- that's what I had.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you have? Tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had-- oh-- postcards that were written by my sister and my father. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what else did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this picture of my sister and my niece and her friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to hide them? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I did hide them when I knew that we were going to have an inspection, because we weren't allowed to have anything like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many times a day or week did you have an inspection?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we never knew, you know. We'd like find out in the last minute. Now, I wouldn't say days a week. I don't know, maybe a couple, few times a month. It depends, you know, whether they were just talking to the commandant or whether they wanted to see the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were-- were there appels in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the appel.

speaker: interviewee
text: 6 o'clock in the morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, snow, rain, heat, whatever, you stood out there, and they counted. And if they were missing, they counted again. And if they were missing, they counted again.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long could this take?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sometimes an hour, hour and a half. Oh, and by then, we wore sort of a uniform already. It was like a-- made like of a material like a sack. And it was like a wrap-around thing that had a hole on the side where you put a thing and-- like an apron. And you tied it in the back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it marked in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. You know what they called us? Political prisons. That's the way they had us--

speaker: interviewer
text: Listed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Because we had the red stripe, and then a yellow triangle. And I think we had the number over it. But the red stripe meant political.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other types of prisoners there that wore a different color stripe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that-- not that I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any Gypsies or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but we got a whole bunch of Hungarian Jewish girls. But that was later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Later. All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened if you got sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, if God helped you, that you got well on your own, you were OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there an infirmary or a clinic, or--?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the first one. Then-- it wasn't an infirmary. But like I said, she'd go out and get medication, you know. Oh, I think we did have a doctor coming in occasionally.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: But not at [? Haza ?]. [? Haza ?] was just-- forget it.

speaker: interviewer
text: At [? Haza, ?] did the women try to entertain each other and keep their morale up there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We all did. Yeah, we all did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you used to talk about at the end of the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: About home, about the people we used to know at-- you know, from--

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because most of us were more or less from the same-- not-- not necessarily from the city, but around there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Same area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. What did the women do to help each other? Was there anything that you did for each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we tired.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: The best we could. If somebody got sick, if you-- if you could share some-- maybe some food or some-- which, believe me, it didn't happen too often. Because I think the human nature is 'I'm first.' That's what I-- I witnessed it.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: But that's later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Later on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any outside visitors to your camp, any-- or people or organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But I'll tell you one thing that I had witnessed with the Germans, with the Nazis, with the big shots, the SS, the Gestapo. The civilian people had no jurisdiction over us. They were not allowed into the camp-- only the ones in uniform.

speaker: interviewer
text: But this--

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you understand what I'm--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, yes. So it was a military--

speaker: interviewee
text: So in other words, we-- we didn't have any fears that something could happen to us from the people on the outside, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why would you have fears of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, who knows what they were capable? They were capable of doing anything and everything. We were afraid of everything. I'm still afraid of a lot of things, believe me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of those civilian workers ever help you in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a relationship with a man that-- he was a mechanic. And he was fixing the machines when something went wrong. I think he was a-- what they call a Social Democrat in those days. And he really-- he was-- he really took his life in his own hands, because he was so good to us. He-- he, you know, got to know a few of us. And you-- you can't trust-- I mean, we wouldn't have squealed, but some people just-- I don't know. They can-- it's not that you can't trust them, but they can say things that they may be sorry for, you know what I'm-- And he would get behind the machine, where he-- nobody could see him. And he would speak to us. He would be telling us how the war's, you know, going. And he would try and bring us some food. And then if he heard that somebody was sick and needed a medication, he tried to get it. We loved him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I forgot. But you know what we called him? We couldn't refer to him by name. Because if we were overhead, you know, he would have been-- we called him Uncle. But we called him in Polish. And in Polish, 'uncle' is 'wujek.' But we gave him an endearing-- Vuichu, which is a-- and that's how we referred to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he outlived his years. And, you know, he was an older man. But he was-- he was really something. He was really and truly something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone ever come to inspect your camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I can't remember names. Oh, yeah, constantly.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. You mentioned that you had seen some high Nazis come to inspect the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the-- that was the good one yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Himmler was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Himmler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many times did you see him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think just that one time. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When they made these inspections, did you have to do anything special?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Just stand. Nothing. He didn't talk to us. He looked-- you know, looked around, looked at the-- whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: As the time went by, what did you hear about what was happening with the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's what-- that's where Vuichu came in, because he kept giving us reports. Because he was listening to the British stations.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the fighting coming closer to your camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know something? Not really. Because I think-- well, in the Sudetenland, where we were-- I don't know. I don't think we were anywhere too close to railroad tracks or anything like this. It was strictly factories, all textile factories. But we knew that, you know-- that Hitler was losing. We just didn't know how long and what-- how long it would take. And then like girls got sick. They used to have-- we used to have selections in our camp. They  used to send them to Auschwitz. We knew that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know about Auschwitz at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. By then, I think we knew already. Now, maybe not exactly how and what they-- how they exterminated. But we knew Auschwitz. We knew the word Auschwitz. And everybody tried to-- the best you could to stay-- stay-- if you stayed well enough to do your work and survive on what they gave you, survive the beatings, survive the-- the things, the hangings, the shootings, whatever they did to the other people, they left you alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever punished?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to an extent.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you beaten?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. I got my licks.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the most vivid memory that you have of the two camps-- or the two sleeping areas that you were in and the working area? What's the most vivid memory of your camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, most-- the one in the first camp, I am so scared of doctors to this day that I cannot begin to tell you. That examination, supposedly, that physical-- that was so vivid in my mind that I see myself standing there now and them looking at me and telling me to turn around. And on that basis, they decided whether I should live or die. And I have such fear of doctors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. To this day. I have a lot of fears. I have a tremendous fear of authority. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: But that's another story. Was there ever a time during those camp years that you lost your faith?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. At times, we didn't think we were going to make it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What helped you to survive during those years?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know whether the will, the will to live-- but I think deep down, we all knew already by 1944 that we had nothing to go home to. I'm not saying that everybody-- some people did have family left. But what is very much outstanding in my mind-- 1945, when the-- at the beginning of 1945 when the Russians were advancing, the Germans were sending the Jews from Auschwitz. They were sending them-- the marches into Germany. And one transport-- all men-- it was the first time I saw a Jewish man since 1941. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They came into our camp-- not for their benefit, because the SS marched them-- with them. And they needed a rest. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I have never seen-- [SOB]-- we looked next to them like-- like we just got back from a resort or something. And we tried to help them, you know, give them whatever we had. And we didn't have a lot, believe me. We didn't have enough for ourselves. But when we saw them, they were emaciated. They couldn't walk. And we saw-- the first time I saw the uniforms that they wore, the stripes. And we got orders-- you are not to help these people. Anybody caught will get shot. They did shoot a few people. Because we just-- and I-- I have never seen-- they were-- the prisoners, they were killing each other to catch a piece of bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did they stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: A couple of days, until the SS rested up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any attempts of suicide in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- till the liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: May 9.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any changes that were happening that made you realize that liberation was close?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I've got to tell you something very, very interesting. See, they ran out of the raw material. Because Russia was moving and taking back their territory. We had nothing to work with. Excuse me. We had nothing to work with. And they couldn't see us sitting idle in the camps. They took us out to dig ditches in an open field.

speaker: interviewer
text: For what purpose?

speaker: interviewee
text: No purpose-- just so we should do something. And we were-- only women-- SS women. And I'm telling you, they were worse than men. And we're standing there. We're digging with shovels. We're digging. We don't know what we're digging, but we're digging. And we were watched. All of a sudden, we hear planes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Airplanes?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in an open field. So how can you not, you know, lift your head and look? And we couldn't believe what we saw-- leaflets-- millions-- coming down. Right away, we hear [GERMAN]. Anybody picking up-- I spoke German, believe it or not. I don't speak it anymore. Anybody picking up one of those gets shot on the spot.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you know something? So many came down that you couldn't help it. Because they  were all over you. We got a few into camp. And this was, I think, about a week or two weeks before the end of the war. What did the leaflet say?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. It was addressed to the German people. I don't remember-- I wish I had held onto one. I wish that I had held onto one. You killed enough. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Stop your killing. You can't bring back the ones you killed. Stop your killing now. Because you are really going to suffer consequences and whatever. And it was signed Roosevelt, Stalin, and Churchill. That's when we knew that-- oh, how I wish I had-- but you know, who thinks, you know, at a time like this? So you felt the end was coming near?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And then, I think, about a week later-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] get up one morning, and we don't see any SS women-- nobody. And this man, this Vuichu, the German soldiers were retreating with their guns and whatever. And we were, by that time, I would say, at least 2,000 girls in that camp. Because they brought in a huge amount of the Hungarian in 1944, end of 1944 when Eichmann came into Hungary. And funny thing is, I think they brought him into us from Auschwitz. Because they came with their hair shaved off. See, we always had our hair. Unfortunately, it wasn't always clean, believe me. And we were all young women. And we were afraid to be there alone. Like I said, they-- no civilian had any jurisdiction over us as long as the Nazis, the SS, was in control.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you left--

speaker: interviewee
text: About three days.

speaker: interviewer
text: --without anyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Without anyone. So he got a couple of his friends, and they had guns. And they stood at the gate of our camp. And they guarded us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about food? What did you do for food at that time? You know, I don't remember. I just don't remember. Were the townspeople so antagonistic towards you that you were afraid of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: The who? The Germans? The--

speaker: interviewer
text: The people that lived in the area.

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't so much even the people that lived in the area. It was the soldiers. They were drunk. They were retreating, you know. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean the German soldiers--

speaker: interviewee
text: Solider.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that were retreating?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who liberated you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about that moment of liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll tell you what I remember. It must have been about 4 o'clock in the afternoon on May 9. The war was officially over May 8. And Vuichu came around, and he got a few of us together. He didn't have any children. And he obviously talked to his wife about us all through-- over the years. He says, my wife made dinner for you. He says, you're coming with me to my house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were people leaving and walking out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They were beginning to-- yeah, to walk out. So-- I don't know-- four, five of us. I can't remember. And we went to his house. And she made-- we were walking. And as we were walking, we heard like a roar-- a roar-- noise, noise, noise. The Russians were coming in with the tanks. And of course, we stopped, and we knew-- and we all knew one   Russian word, [RUSSIAN], which is hello, you know. And-- we wore our Star of David.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you wearing your uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. We-- of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your uniform also?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember whether we-- I had the-- maybe I had taken it off and put it on, something else, whatever I had. I don't remember. But we wanted them to know that we're Jewish. And they came in, and they-- they occupied.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they treat the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they came into the camps. See, we were at his house. And this woman saved us some chocolate. She knew that we wouldn't-- we didn't have it all these years. So she saved us some chocolate. We came back to camp. And I want to tell you something. The Russians brought in a lot of food and booze, you know. They were known for that. And the girls were having a ball-- a party.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they mistreat the prisoners in any way? Or how did they treat the prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which prisoners?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, the former prisoners.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you mean us girls?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. They were very good to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you were afraid of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they have-- they had a reputation of rape.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, a couple of-- there were a couple of incidents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any Jewish liberators among them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in the upper-- the officers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they speak to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was their reaction when they saw you? How much did you weigh at liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: We weren't that-- like you see the other people. Like I said, if you did what they wanted, the work that they gave you, and you could live on what they gave you-- and it also depends on one's makeup or whatever. No, we-- overall, we didn't look too bad. We didn't look like some of these people that came out of the camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Let's talk about you particularly. Did you need any medical care at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go and what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed in the camp. We stayed in the camp. But Czech government took over, you know. They came in-- back to power. And they decided they didn't want us there. Go back home where you came from. And they provided us with transportation. And went back to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: About a month after. Because, you know, it was also that the Russians were saying to the girls, take us anyplace you want to, anybody's apartment, and take anything you want-- jewelry, furs, or whatever-- which was OK to go into Germans' homes, but not to Czech homes. Because they suffered, too, you know. They suffered, too. So they got a little fed up with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to any of the guards that were caught?

speaker: interviewee
text: They ran away.

speaker: interviewer
text: They all ran away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes. Some of them, they caught. And the girls dealt with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shaved their heads, beat them up, maybe killed one or two. They did. They did. There was retaliation, definitely.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you returned to Sosnowiec?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It took me a week on the cattle trains. Because the-- it was chaos-- chaos-- traveling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any money?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I think they gave us some. [BACKGROUND NOISE] I think they gave us some. I can't remember. I came back. I went back to Sosnowiec. And at that time, they had already established a-- a committee, a Jewish committee. Because, I-- well, first of all, I was liberated a day later than anybody else, which wasn't really that much of a difference. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you find when you returned home?

speaker: interviewee
text: What did I find? I went to my home, my apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it there?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, by Pollocks. And Hitler didn't kill you yet?

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the reaction?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That was the reaction. Hitler didn't kill you? I knew I'm not going to live there. I knew it. So we went together, a few girls that we-- you know, a group that we stuck together over the years. And I don't know how we got an apartment, a room, or something. And we moved in. I don't even know how we lived. Maybe we got money from the committee, the Jewish committee. I really don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. And we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me just ask you about your family. Were you able to find out what happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, so I came into this committee. I registered. And I wanted to know, did anybody-- stop.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you find anybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at that point.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. How long did you stay in Sosnowiec?

speaker: interviewee
text: About six weeks-- between four and six weeks. And a whole bunch of us picked ourselves up and, ironically, went back to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: For what purpose?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because all the action was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. We're coming to the end of the tape. We're going to take a moment to change tapes.
